**Updates in Version 2.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- First line 

- �Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy + Pembrolizumab changed to a category 1 recommendation. 

- �Datopotamab deruxtecan-dlnk changed to a preferred recommendation.

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include: General**
content: - References were updated throughout.

- Workup, last bullet added: Assess for distress (Also for IBC-1) 

- Footnote f added: Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. (Also for BINV-1, PHYLL-1, PREG-1, PAGET-1, IBC-1) 

- Footnote h revised: Surgical axillary staging should not be performed for preoperative (biopsy-determined) pure DCIS unless there is some clinical- _radiographic_ -pathologic suggestion of invasion or axillary metastasis. _SLNB may be considered in 1) concern for persistently positive margins 2) excision in an anatomic location compromising the performance of a future SLNB procedure; or 3) oncoplastic breast-conserving surgery (see BINV-D for considerations for SLNB)._ 

- Footnote i revised: ~~An SLNB may be considered in the setting of 1) mastectomy for DCIS., 2) excision in an anatomic location compromising the performance of a future SLNB procedure 3) oncoplastic breast conserving surgery (see BINV-D for appropriate candidates for SLNB).~~ _Omission of SLNB in the setting of mastectomy may be considered in patients >50 years with nonpalpable low-grade ER-positive DCIS with no abnormal axillary findings and in patients in whom the primary reason for mastectomy is patient preference._ 

- Footnote n revised: Whole breast RT following BCS reduces ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (IBTR) rates ~~in~~ _for all_ DCIS by 50%–70%. Approximately half of the recurrences are invasive and half are DCIS. The risk of local relapse increases with larger DCIS, palpable mass, grade III disease, margins ≤2 mm, ER-negative tumors, and age <50. ~~Select patients with "low" risk DCIS may be considered suitable for omission of RT (endocrine therapy alone, if all RTOG 9804 criteria are met: screen-detected, grade 1–2, size ≤2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm).~~ _Omission of RT and treatment with endocrine therapy alone may be considered if all RTOG 9804 criteria are met (screen-detected, grade 1–2, size ≤2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm)._
- DCIS Postsurgical Treatment, header revised: Risk reduction therapy for ipsilateral breast ~~following BCS~~

- Workup 

- �Bullet 2, sub-bullet 2 revised: _Breast_ ultrasound as necessary 

- �Bullet 2, sub-sub-bullet added: Axillary ultrasound if considering omission of surgical nodal staging 

- cT1-4 pathway, last column, bullet 1 revised: BCS ± surgical axillary staging ~~+~~ _±_ ~~whole breast~~ RT 

- Footnote b added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-negative, grade 1–2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with lobular histology. (Also for BINV-2)

- Header revised: Locoregional Treatment of cT1–3, cN0 or CN+, M0 Disease: Breast-Conserving Surgery (BCS) ~~+~~ _±_ ~~Whole Breast~~ RT 

- Column 3 header revised: _Pathologic_ Nodal Status 

- Column 4, last pathway, option revised: Consider APBI/PBI ~~in~~ _for appropriately_ selected patients who are low risk (category 1) 

- Footnote r added: Based on data from the Europa trial (Meattini I, et al. Lancet 2025;26;37-50), older patients eligible for omission of RT may choose to receive RT alone instead of endocrine therapy with no adverse effects on _early_ breast cancer outcomes. 

- • Footnote removed: _Sentinel node biopsy may be omitted based on the SSO Choosing Wisely recommendation in patients ≥70 years of age with HR+/HER2-negative and pT1, cN0 tumors (Hughes KS, et al. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:2382-2387. Kunkler IH, et al. N Engl J Med 2023:388:585-594)._

- Column 3 

- �Top pathway revised: Negative axillary nodes _(pN0/pN0[i+])_ and ≤pT2 (≤5 cm) and _negative_ margins ~~≥1 mm~~ 

- �Bottom pathway revised: _Any pT/pN with positive margins_ ~~Margins positive~~ 

- �Pathway removed: Negative axillary nodes and ≤pT2 (≤5 cm) and negative margins but <1 mm. 

- Footnote x revised: In the case of _pN1mi (_ a micrometastasis [>0.2 to ≤2.0 mm] _)_ , and no axillary dissection, evaluate other patient risk factors when considering RT. 

- Footnote y revised: PMRT may be considered for patients with multiple high-risk recurrence factors, including central/medial tumors or tumors ≥2 cm and at least one of the following: grade 3, ER-negative, _young age/longevity_ , or LVI. 

- Footnote z added: In the case of pT3N0, high-risk features for considering PMRT include, but are not limited to, young age/longevity and/or LVI.

- Footnote mm revised: Updated results from the adjuvant APHINITY trial in HER2-positive early breast cancer, with a median follow-up of ~~8.4~~ _11.3_ years, have confirmed the benefit of adding Pertuzumab to Trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in preventing recurrences. (Also for subsequent pages) 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- Additional Workup 

- �Bullet 1, sub-bullet revised: ~~Consider~~ _Axillary_ ultrasound _unless prior MRI demonstrated negative axillary nodes_ 

- Footnote tt revised: ~~If considering preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for postmenopausal patients with cN0, operable ERpositive, HER2-negative disease (Iwata H, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2019;173:123-133; Pease AM, et al. Ann Surg Oncol 2019;26:366-371).~~ If considering preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for pre- and postmenopausal patients with cN0 and postmenopausal patients with cN1, operable ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. 

- **BINV-14**

- �Top pathway revised: Adjuvant systemic therapy _(complete planned regimen course if not completed preoperatively)_ + whole breast RT 

   - Bullet 2 revised: cN _1_ and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed ~~_._ ; and strongly consider C~~ omprehensive RNI _is not routinely indicated_ ~~with inclusion~~ 

   - ~~of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk.~~ 

   - ◊ Bullet 3 added: cN2–3 and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed with comprehensive RNI including any portion of the axilla at risk. ◊ Bullet 4 revised: Any ypN+: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed ~~; and~~ ~~_w_~~ _ith_ comprehensive RNI ~~with inclusion of~~ _including_ any portion of the ~~undissected~~ axilla at risk. 

   - _by SLNB, TAD, or ALND)_ 

   - Bullet 1 added: cT1–T3, cN0, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated. 

   - Bullet 2 added: cT1–T3, cN1, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated. 

   - Bullet 3 added: cN2–3 and ypN0; any ypN+; cT4 any N: PMRT + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the axilla at risk. 

   - Bottom pathway removed: Adjuvant systemic therapy, no PMRT 

   - Sub-bullets removed: 

   - cN+ and ypN0: Strongly consider PMRT to the chest wall and comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk. 

   - **–** Any ypN+: PMRT is indicated to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk. 

   - **–** cT4, any N: RT is indicated to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk. 

   - **–** cT3, cN0, and ypN0: Consider PMRT to the chest wall + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk. 

- Footnote xx revised: ~~Complete planned systemic therapy regimen course, if not completed preoperatively.~~ _Among patients shown to be N+ prior to preoperative systemic therapy, SLNB has a >10% false-negative rate when performed after preoperative systemic therapy, which can be improved by marking the most suspicious biopsied node prior to treatment, and at the time of surgery, using dual tracers, obtaining ≥3 sentinel lymph nodes nodes and removal of the marked node (Targeted Axillary Dissection [TAD])._ 

- • Footnote yy revised: Strongly consider RT boost for high-risk features ( ~~eg, high-grade disease, age <50 years~~ _central/medial tumors, tumors ≥2 cm, with ≥1 of the following: grade 3, ER-negative, LVI, high residual cancer in the breast, or young age/longevity_ ). 

- Footnote zz revised: _When considering PMRT + RNI for cN1 that has become ypN0 (after preoperative therapy), based on limited followup duration of NSABP B-51 study (Mamounas EP, et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2113-2124) and the inability to demonstrate long-term non-inferiority for this subgroup, a discussion of  potential risks of undertreatment should be weighed against the previously established long-term survival benefits of PMRT+RNI, particularly for patients of younger age/longevity with ER-positive tumors, residual disease in the breast, and/or those with multiple traditional clinical pathologic high-risk features._ ~~(Mamounas E, Bandos H, White J, et al. Loco-regional irradiation in patients with biopsy-proven axillary node Involvement at presentation who become pathologically node-negative after neoadjuvant chemotherapy: Primary outcomes of NRG Oncology/NSABP B-51/RTOG 1304; Abstract GS02-07; SABCS 2023.)~~ 

- **BINV-16** • Top pathway, column 2 revised: Adjuvant endocrine therapy (category 1) _, consider ribociclib for eligible patients, see BINV-K 2 of 3 for eligibility criteria._ • ypT1-4,N0 or ypN≥1 pathway revised to include: "or fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (category 1) for those with high risk of recurrence (see BINV-M 4 of 10 for high-risk criteria)" 

- **BINV-17** 

- Breast imaging 

- �Bullet 2 revised: Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated _after mastectomy_ 

- Lifestyle 

- �Bullet 2 added: Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with caution in those with increased genetic cancer risk. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship. 

- �Footnote ddd revised: Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and RT with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance. Suspicious findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging _should warrant immediate follow-up/workup_ ~~might warrant a shorter interval between mammograms.~~ 

**Continued UPDATES**

**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content: - Biomarker testing, sub-bullet 3 revised: ~~Comprehensive germline and somatic profling~~ _Multigene panel testing (MGPT) (somatic and germline)_ to identify candidates for

- Footnote kkk revised: I ~~n the setting of oligometastatic breast cancer, the currently available data do not support ablative metastasis-directed RT (ie, stereotactic body radiation therapy [SBRT]) for extending overall survival (OS) or progression-free survival (PFS). In some cases, SBRT may be preferred over palliative RT to provide more durable local control and pain relief.~~ _Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms. Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term diseasefree survival (DFS) for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to “no evidence of disease” (NED). For de novo oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases, and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be considered._ (Also for BINV-21) 

- **BINV-19** 

- Column 3 

- �3rd row revised: _Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated then_ surgical resection if feasible + consider surgical axillary staging + PMRT 

- �4th row revised: _Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated then_ surgical resection if feasible + _consider_ surgical axillary staging if + repeat RT, if feasible and indicated 

- Column 4 revised: Consider appropriate _adjuvant_ systemic therapy _as clinically indicated_ and Supportive Care and see Principles of Survivorship (Also for BINV-20) 

- Footnote removed: Consider systemic therapy to best response, then resect if possible. (Also for subsequent pages) 

- Footnote removed: See the Discussion for additional information. (Also for subsequent pages)

- 1st row revised: _Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated_ , then surgical resection if feasible with ALND (for axillary recurrence) + RT if feasible 

- 2nd row revised: _Consider appropriate systemic therapy to best response if indicated, then_ surgical resection of gross disease ± ALND (for axillary recurrence) if feasible + RT if feasible

- Footnote removed: Routine surgical resection of the primary breast tumor is generally not indicated in the management of patients presenting with de novo stage IV (M1) disease. Although there is no survival benefit, it may be considered for local control of the primary tumor. Discussion regarding management of the primary tumor in this setting must be individualized. 

- **BINV-22** 

- Section significantly revised.

- Terminology was changed from systemic therapy to cytotoxic therapy. (Also for subsequent pages) 

- Column 2, revised: If not endocrine therapy refractory, consider: Alternate endocrine therapy ± targeted therapy _(if premenopausal, consider ovarian • ablation or suppression)_ . 

- Footnote ttt added: Cytotoxic therapy includes chemotherapy or antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs), or targeted therapy. (Also for subsequent pages) **BINV-A**

- Clinical Indications and Applications � Bullet 1 revised: May be used for staging evaluation to define extent of cancer or presence of multifocal or multicentric cancer in the ipsilateral breast, or as screening of the contralateral breast cancer at time of initial diagnosis (category 2B). ~~There are no high-level data to demonstrate that the use of MRI to facilitate local therapy~~ 

   - ~~decision-making improves local recurrence or survival~~ _Available data does not demonstrate that the use of preoperative MRI improves re-excision rates, local recurrence, or survival for DCIS or invasive breast cancer in patients pursuing BCS_ . 

**Continued** 

**UPDATES**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- Right column 

- �Bullet 2 revised: Alternative methods of birth control include _non-hormonal_ –based intrauterine devices (IUDs), barrier methods, or, for patients with no intent of future pregnancies, tubal ligation or vasectomy for the partner 

- �Last bullet revised: Breastfeeding ~~following~~ _after_ breast-conservation _therapy_ ~~cancer treatment i~~ s not contraindicated...

- Column 1, top pathway added: No palpable lymph node at diagnosis and negative axillary ultrasound 

- SLN positive pathway 

- �Bullet 1 revised: cT1–T2, T3h cN0 ~~or pN1mic~~ 

- �Bullet 3 revised: _pN1mic;_ 1–2 positive SLNs 

- Footnote e added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2negative, grade 1–2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with lobular histology.

- Footnote added: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2negative, grade 1–2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with lobular histology. 

- Footnote removed: Sentinel node biopsy may be omitted based on the SSO Choosing Wisely recommendation in patients ≥70 years of age with HR+/HER2-negative and pT1, cN0 tumors _(Hughes KS, et al. J Clin Oncol 2013;31:2382-2387. Kunkler IH, et al. N Engl J Med 2023:388:585-594)_ . 

- Footnote b removed: Based on emerging data from the SOUND trial, patients with HR+/HER2-negative tumors, pT1, N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), may be considered for omission of SLN biopsy. Gentilini OD, et al. JAMA Oncol 2023;9:1557-1564.

- "For BCS" added to Absolute and Relative contraindications 

- Absolute contraindications, bullet 5 revised: Patients diagnosed with gestational breast cancer _(not receiving systemic chemotherapy)_ who cannot receive RT within 12–16 weeks; see PREG-1 

- Absolute contraindications, bullet 6 revised: Multicentric disease with any of the following criteria _(mandate mastectomy)_

- Bullet 7revised: Skin- _and nipple-_ sparing mastectomy should be performed by an experienced breast surgery team that works in a coordinated, multidisciplinary fashion to guide proper patient selection for skin-sparing mastectomy, determine optimal sequencing of the reconstructive procedure(s) in relation to adjuvant therapies, and perform a resection that achieves appropriate surgical margins. 

- Last bullet added: Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy but diagnostic imaging may be required for new symptomatic concerns. **BINV-I (1 of 3)** 

- Optimizing Delivery of Individual Therapy, 

- �Bullet 1, sub-bullet 5 last sentence added: Consider daily imaging for patients receiving prone breast radiotherapy. 

- Whole Breast RT, bullet 2, sub-bullet 3 revised: _Available data support the use of_ ultra-hypofractionated _regimens_ ~~whole breast RT of (2~~ 8.5 Gy in 5 ~~(o~~ nce-aweek fractions ~~)~~ _or 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions). The optimal way to incorporate a tumor bed boost and the long-term toxicity associated with its delivery in the ultrahypofractionated setting is yet to be established._ ~~fractions may be considered for selected patients over 50 years following BCS with early-stage, node-negative disease, particularly those in whom a boost is not intended.~~ 

- Footnote c added: Daily image guidance in conjunction with regular 3D assessments (eg, cone beam CT, SGRT) for IMRT/VMAT techniques. 

- Footnote removed: Alternatively, 26 Gy in 5 daily fractions over one week may be considered, though data beyond 5 years for local relapse or toxicity are not yet available for this regimen. (Murray Brunt A, Haviland JS, Wheatley DA, et al. Hypofractionated breast radiotherapy for 1 week versus 3 weeks [FAST-Forward]: 5-year efficacy and late normal tissue effects results from a multicentre, non-inferiority, randomised, phase 3 trial. Lancet 2020;395:1613-1626.) 

- Footnote removed: Brunt AM, Haviland JS, Sydenham M, et al. Ten-year results of FAST: A randomized controlled trial of 5-fraction whole-breast radiotherapy for early breast cancer. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:3261-3272. 

**Continued**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- PMRT (including breast reconstruction) 

- �Last bullet added: For patients with positive surgical margins after neoadjuvant systemic therapy, re-excision to negative margins is preferred. If not feasible, then strongly consider PMRT to chest wall ± comprehensive RNI (including any portion of the undissected axilla at risk). 

- �Bullet removed: In the case of cT3N0, high-risk features for considering PMRT include, but are not limited to, young age and/or LVI. 

- RNI 

- �Bullet 3 added: RNI generally includes supraclavicular, infraclavicular, internal mammary and axillary nodes at risk. Coverage of the internal mammary nodes may be individually determined based on tumor location (medial/central), tumor size, extent of nodal involvement, and dose to the OAR. 

- �Bullet 4, sub-bullet 1 revised: RT doses to the regional nodes of 46–50 Gy (conventional fractionation) or 39–42 _.56_ Gy (moderately fractionated) dosing schedules _are_ similar to PMRT and whole breast ~~may be considered~~ . 

- Footnote d added: Bolus should be used for patients with skin involvement, positive superficial margins, and those with dermal lymphatic invasion and/or extensive LVI. 

- �Footnote e added: Preliminary data show similar normal tissue toxicity at 5 years with 26 Gy in 5 fractions, for patients receiving regional nodal irradiation without inclusion of internal mammary nodes. Longer-term toxicity and efficacy data are not yet available, and limited data are available in the context of reconstruction. (UK FF Nodal Sub-study; Murray Brunt et al, Rad Onc 2025).

- Bullet 1 revised: The NCCN Panel endorses APBI/PBI for any patient without germline BRCA1/2 PVs who meets the criteria outlined in the ~~2023~~ _2024_ ASTRO guidelines. Patients aged ≥40 years are recommended for APBI/PBI if they have: 

- **BINV-K (1 of 4)** 

- General Principles 

�Bullet 1, sub-bullet 1 revised: ER+ tumors: ER testing should be used to determine if a patient is a candidate for endocrine therapies. Patients with cancers with 1%–100% ER IHC staining are considered ER+ and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are limited efficacy data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-lowpositive (1%–10%) results. ~~The ER-low-positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus, individualized consideration of risks versus benefts of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-making.~~ _High ER expression is more likely to benefit from endocrine therapy; however, the potential benefit of endocrine therapy should be discussed even with low ER expression._ �Bullet 3, sub-bullet 1 revised: Level of ER expression: ~~Low ER+ expression is less likely to beneft from endocrine therapy.~~ _High ER+ expression is more likely to benefit from endocrine therapy; however, the potential benefit of endocrine therapy should be discussed even with low ER expression._ 

- �Footnotes from subsequent pages were added as bullets 4, 5, and 6.

- Ovarian function assessment 

- �Bullet 2 revised: Monitor estradiol ~~and~~ _±_ follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)/luteinizing hormone (LH) levels: 

- �Bullet 2, last sub-bullet revised: ~~Prior to next dose of GNRH agonist, particularly in women under 45 years. F~~ requency of testing of estradiol ~~and~~ _±_ FSH/LH levels should be individualized. 

- �Last bullet added: If patient is not postmenopausal, sequential evaluation of hormonal status is recommended. 

- Methods for OFS, bullet 1, sub-bullet 3 revised: Triptorelin: 3.75 mg IM every 4 weeks _or 11.25 mg IM every 12 weeks_ . 

- Header revised: _Options for_ Initiation of OFS 

- �Bullet 1 revised: _Prior to or_ with start of chemotherapy ( ~~neoadjuvant~~ _preoperative_ or adjuvant) 

- Duration of OFS 

- �Bullet 1 revised: 5 years of OFS is optimal based on SOFT and TEXT trials. ~~The 8-year DFS was 85.4% with OFS + Tamoxifen versus 80.2% with Tamoxifen alone.~~ T _he 15-year DFS in SOFT trial was 73.5% with OFS + exemestane versus 70.5% with OFS + tamoxifen versus 67.0% for tamoxifen alone._ A minimum 2 years of OFS is encouraged. There are no data on efficacy or safety to support prolonging of OFS >5 years. 

**Continued**

**Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- Column 1 revised: Premenopausal at diagnosis _(if postmenopausal at diagnosis)_ (Also for BINV-K 4) 

- Column 4, header added: Extended Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy (Also for BINV-K 4)
- Section significantly revised.

- Row 1, column 3, last sentence added: Non-randomized data suggest that patients with MammaPrint-high 2 (MP-H2) tumors have significantly worse outcomes when treated with taxane-based regimens without anthracyclines compared to MP-H1 tumors, suggesting benefit of addition of anthracyclines to taxane-based chemotherapy in MP-H2 tumors.

- Row 1, column 3, pN0 header added. 

- Row 1, column 3, pN1 (1–3 positive nodes) added: For patients with HR-positive, HER2-negative, and pN1 tumors (1–3 positive lymph nodes), an optimized BCI model integrating tumor size and grade is prognostic for distant recurrence at 10 years. 

- Row 2, column 3 

- �Bullet 1 revised: For patients with _p_ T1 HR-positive, HER2-negative, and pN0 tumors or _pN1 tumors (1–3 positive lymph nodes)_ , a BCI (H/I) high (5.1–10) 

   - demonstrated significant rates of late distant recurrence. 

- �Bullet 2 revised: In secondary analyses of the MA.17, Trans-aTTom, and IDEAL trials, patients with HR-positive, _p_ T1–T3, pN0 _or pN1 tumors (1–3 positive lymph nodes)_ ~~or pN+~~ who had a BCI (H/I) high demonstrated significant improvements in DFS when adjuvant endocrine therapy was extended, compared to the control arm. 

**BINV-P (2 of 3)** 

- First-Line Therapy 

- �Last bullet moved from Useful in Certain Circumstances and revised to: For HER2-negative tumors with PIK3CA activating mutations: 

   - Sub-bullet added: Fulvestrant/Inavolisib/Palbociclib, see BINV-Q (6). 

- Useful in Certain Circumstances for Second- and/or Subsequent-Line Therapy 

- �Bullet 3 added: Imlunestrant + Abemaciclib

- Row 1, sentence added: Aromatase inhibitor ± Palbociclib + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab 

- Footnote m added: The combination of imlunestrant + abemaciclib may be most effective in those with ESR1 mutations and no prior CDK4/6 inhibitor. The EMBER-3 trial (Jhaveri KL, et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:1189-1202) included patients with and without ESR1 mutations and compared the combination of imlunestrant + abemaciclib to imlunestrant alone versus comparing with the standard treatment containing an endocrine therapy plus CDK4/6 inhibitor.

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- Header revised: ~~Systemic~~ _Cytotoxic_ Therapy for Recurrent Unresectable (Local or Regional) or Stage IV (M1) Disease 

- General considerations, bullets added: 

- The selection, dosing, and administration of anti-cancer agents and the management of associated toxicities are complex. Modifications of drug dose and schedule and initiation of supportive care interventions are often necessary because of expected toxicities and individual patient variability, prior treatment, and comorbidity. The optimal delivery of anti-cancer agents therefore requires a health care delivery team experienced in the use of anti-cancer agents and the management of associated toxicities in patients with cancer. 

- �For information regarding DPYD testing, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer. 

- �Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa- 

   - pmph has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab. 

- �Bullet 6, sub-bullet 2 revised: Consider cryotherapy of hands and feet to decrease the risk of peripheral neuropathy. Use of glove constriction may be considered as it helps with reducing peripheral neuropathy. 

- For Those Receiving HER2-Targeted Therapy 

- �Bullet 2 revised: ~~Pertuzumab, Trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV Pertuzumab and Trastuzumab; however, the dosing and administration instruction may difer.~~ _Pertuzumab, trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf injection for subcutaneous use may be substituted anywhere that the combination of intravenous pertuzumab and intravenous trastuzumab are given as part of systemic therapy. Pertuzumab, trastuzumab, and hyaluronidase-zzxf injection for subcutaneous use has different dosing and administration instructions compared to the intravenous products._ 

- **BINV-Q (3 of 15)** 

- First line, row 1: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy + Pembrolizumab was added as a category 2A, preferred recommendation. 

- First line, row 2: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy was added as a category 1, preferred recommendation. 

- First line, row 2: Datopotamab deruxtecan-dlnk was added as a category 2A, other recommended recommendation. 

- Footnote k added: See NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors.

- First line row 1, revised: Docetaxel + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab (category 1, preferred) _with maintenance Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab If HR-positive: Aromatase inhibitor ± Palbociclib + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab_ 

- First line row 2, revised: Paclitaxel + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab (preferred) _with maintenance Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab If HR-positive: Aromatase inhibitor ± Palbociclib + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab_ 

- First line row 3: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki + Pertuzumab was added as a category 2A, other recommended recommendation. 

- Second/Third Line row combined. 

- Footnote o added: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is associated with ILD/pneumonitis. Regular monitoring for this serious side effect is recommended. For patients 

- with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing safety or toxicity of this drug in a trial. 

- Footnote q revised: _Single-agent_ fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki may be considered in the first-line setting as an option for select patients (ie, those with rapid progression within 6 months of neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy [12 months for Pertuzumab-containing regimens]). ~~Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is associated~~ 

- ~~with ILD/pneumonitis. Regular monitoring for this serious side efect is recommended. For patients with a history of ILD/pneumonitis, there are no data on managing safety or toxicity of this drug in a trial.~~ 

- Footnote removed: Maintenance Pertuzumab/Trastuzumab after response (with concurrent endocrine therapy if ER+ and HER2+ metastatic breast cancer).

- Footnote v revised: Assess for ESR1 mutations at progression following prior lines of endocrine therapy (ctDNA preferred). _If ESR1 negative and TF is low, consider reflex testing with tissue-based NGS assay._ 

- Footnote w added: Tissue biopsy is preferred to ctDNA at detecting homozygous copy loss of PTEN or TMB. 

- Footnote removed: The safety of Alpelisib in patients with Type 1 or uncontrolled Type 2 diabetes has not been established. **BINV-Q (7 of 15)** 

- Footnote removed: Tissue biopsy is more sensitive than ctDNA (liquid biopsy) at detecting homozygous copy loss of PTEN or TMB-H. (Also for BINV-Q 7) 

**Continued**

header: **Updates in Version 1.2026 of the NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer include:**

content:
- Row 1 column 4 revised: Tucatinib + Trastuzumab ± Fulvestrant was added as a category 2A, useful in certain circumstances recommendation. **BINV-Q (9 through 15 of 15)** 

- Dosing and references updated.
- Section significantly revised.

- Top pathway, last column revised: Examination or _BI-RADS 4 or 5_ imaging ~~positive for breast lesion.~~ (Also for top pathway, column 1 on PAGET-2) **PAGET-2** 

- Pathways 2 and 3 revised to follow the same pathway as pathways 3 and 4. 

- Column 4 revised: Consider breast MRI and tissue sampling _, if indicated_ 

- Column 5 revised: Central lumpectomy including NAC _± SLNB_ with whole breast RT 

or 

Total mastectomy including NAC ± SLNB with or without breast reconstruction ~~Central lumpectomy including NAC ± SLNB without RT (category 2B)~~

- Footnote a revised: CT scans and nuclear imaging _(bone scan or PET/CT) and contrast-enhanced MRI_ are contraindicated during pregnancy. 

- Footnote removed: Considerations and selection of optimal local therapy and systemic therapy are similar to that recommended in non–pregnancy-associated breast cancer; see other sections of this guideline. However, the selection and timing of chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, and RT is different if pregnant. BCS during the first trimester of pregnancy can be considered in those who will require adjuvant chemotherapy and can have adjuvant RT delayed until after delivery. Chemotherapy should not be administered during the first trimester of pregnancy, and RT should not be administered during any trimester of pregnancy. Coordination is recommended between the oncology and obstetrics teams to plan the optimal timing of systemic therapy administration during pregnancy. Most experience with chemotherapy during pregnancy for breast cancer is from regimens that utilize various combinations of Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, and Fluorouracil. Considerations for postpartum chemotherapy are the same as for non–pregnancy-associated breast cancer. 

- Footnote removed: Use of blue dye is contraindicated in pregnancy; radiolabeled sulfur colloid appears to be safe for SLNB in pregnancy. See Considerations for Surgical Axillary Staging (BINV-D).
- New section added.

- Top pathway, treatment revised: Total mastectomy _(skin-sparing and nipple-sparing mastectomy are contraindicated)_ + level l/ll axillary dissection + RT to chest wall and comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the undissected axilla at risk ± delayed breast reconstruction

**Treatment (DCIS-2)**| - a Encapsulated papillary carcinoma (EPC) without associated conventional invasion is staged as pTis because behavior is similar to DCIS (per AJCC). SPC should be specified as in situ or invasive based on WHO criteria but both forms have favorable outcomes. - b The Panel endorses the College of American Pathologists Protocol for pathology reporting for all invasive and noninvasive carcinomas of the breast. http://www.cap.org. - c For risk criteria, see NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate. - d Principles of Dedicated Breast MRI Testing (BINV-B). - e The use of MRI has not been shown to increase likelihood of negative margins or decrease conversion to mastectomy. Data to support improved long-term outcomes are lacking. - f Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. - g Re-resection(s) may be performed in an effort to obtain negative margins in patients desiring breast-conservation therapy. Patients in whom adequate surgical margins cannot be achieved with BCS should undergo a total mastectomy. For definition of adequate surgical margins, see Margin Status Recommendations After BCS for Invasive Cancers and DCIS (BINV-F). - h Surgical axillary staging should not be performed for preoperative (biopsydetermined) pure DCIS unless there is some clinical-radiographic-pathologic suggestion of invasion or axillary metastasis. SLNB may be considered for 1) concern of persistently positive margins 2) excision in an anatomic location compromising the performance of a future SLNB procedure; or 3) oncoplastic breast-conserving surgery (see BINV-D for considerations for SLNB). - i Omission of SLNB in the setting of mastectomy may be considered in patients >50 years with non-palpable low-grade ER-positive DCIS with no abnormal axillary findings and in patients in whom the primary reason for mastectomy is patient preference. j Principles of Breast Reconstruction Following Surgery (BINV-H). - k Invasive disease at total mastectomy or re-excision should be managed as clinical stage l or stage ll disease (BINV-1). l Principles of Radiation Therapy (BINV-I). - m Contraindications to Breast-Conserving Approaches Requiring RT (BINV-G). - n Whole breast RT following BCS reduces ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (IBTR) rates for all DCIS by 50%–70%. Approximately half of the recurrences are invasive and half are DCIS. The risk of local relapse increases with larger DCIS, palpable mass, grade III disease, margins ≤2 mm, ER-negative tumors, and age <50. Omission of RT and treatment with endocrine therapy alone may be considered if all RTOG 9804 criteria are met (screen-detected, grade 1–2, size ≤2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm). **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** **DCIS-1** Version 2.2026, 02/27/26 © 2026 National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ), All rights reserved. header: **NCCN Guidelines Version 2.2026 Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS)**

content:
- **Consider endocrine therapy for 5 years for patients with ERpositive DCIS, if:** 

- **Treated with BCS and RT[o] (category 1)** 

- **Treated with excision alone[p]** 

- **Endocrine therapy[q] :** 

- **Tamoxifen[o,r] for premenopausal patients** 

- **Tamoxifen[o,r] or aromatase inhibitor for postmenopausal patients with some advantage for aromatase inhibitor therapy in patients <60 years or with concerns for thromboembolism** 

- **Risk reduction therapy for contralateral breast:** 

   - **Interval history and physical exam every 6–12 mo for 5 y, then annually** 

   - **First mammogram 6–12 mo, after end of RT, if given, or after BCS (category 2B) if no RT is given and annually thereafter** 

- **Counseling regarding risk reduction** 

- _CYP2D6_ genotype testing is not recommended for patients considering tamoxifen. 

- p Available data suggest endocrine therapy provides risk reduction in the ipsilateral breast treated with breast conservation and in the contralateral breast in patients with mastectomy or breast conservation with ER-positive primary tumors. Since a survival advantage has not been demonstrated, individual consideration of risks and benefits is important. 

- q The use of a bisphosphonate (PO/IV) or denosumab is acceptable to maintain or improve bone mineral density (BMD) and reduce risk of fractures in postmenopausal (natural or induced) patients receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy. Optimal duration of either therapy has not been established. Benefits from duration beyond 3 years or optimal duration beyond 3 years is not known. Factors to consider for duration of anti-osteoporosis therapy include BMD, response to therapy, and risk factors for continued bone loss or fracture. There are case reports of spontaneous fractures after denosumab discontinuation. Patients treated with a bisphosphonate or denosumab should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to the initiation of therapy, and should take supplemental calcium and vitamin D. 

- r Low-dose tamoxifen (5 mg per day or 10 mg every other day for 3–5 years) is an option if patient is symptomatic on the 20-mg dose or if patient is unwilling or unable to take standard-dose 20 mg per day tamoxifen [10 mg every other day since 5-mg dose is not available in the United States]. This low dosage needs further investigation in premenopausal individuals (DeCensi A, et al. J Clin Oncol 2019;37:1629-1637). 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** 

**DCIS-2**

**Stage IV (M1) Disease(BINV-18)**|| - a For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. - b Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials, patients >50 years and postmenopausal with cT1N0 (node negative by axillary ultrasound), with HR+/HER2-negative, grade 1–2 tumors who are agreeable to receiving whole breast RT and endocrine therapy may be considered for omission of SLNB. Caution should be used in those with lobular histology. - c Breast MRI may be useful for characterizing axillary and/or internal mammary nodal disease. See Principles of Dedicated Breast MRI Testing (BINV-B) and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis. d The Panel endorses the College of American Pathologists Protocol for pathology reporting for all invasive and noninvasive carcinomas of the breast. http://www.cap.org. e Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). f For risk criteria, see NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate. g For Fertility, Birth Control, and Sexual Health, see BINV-C. The general considerations for fertility and sexual health/function outlined for specific populations in NCCN Guidelines for Adolescent and Young Adult (AYA) Oncology and NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship are applicable to all patients diagnosed with breast cancer. - h Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. - i Routine systemic staging is not indicated for non-metastatic (M0) cancer in the absence of systemic symptoms. If metastatic disease is suspected, see Workup on BINV-18. - j Patients with a known genetic predisposition to breast cancer may have an increased risk of contralateral or ipsilateral breast cancers after breast-conservation therapy. Risk reduction strategies including prophylactic mastectomies should be discussed. See NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate.

See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). aa According to WHO, carcinoma of no special type (NST) encompasses multiple patterns including medullary pattern, cancers with neuroendocrine expression, and other rare patterns. e ~~e~~ Although patients with cancers with 1%–100% ER immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining are considered ER-positive and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are more limited data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-low–positive (1%–10%) results. The ER-low–positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus, individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-making. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). ff Definition of Menopause (BINV-O). bb There are rare subtypes of metaplastic carcinoma (low-grade adenosquamous and low-grade fibromatosis-like carcinoma) that are considered to have a favorable prognosis without adjuvant systemic therapies. cc To be associated with favorable prognosis, the favorable histologic type should not be high grade, should be pure (>90% as classified on the surgical excision, not core biopsy alone), and should be HER2 negative. See BINV-I for sequencing of systemic therapy and RT. and - Follow-up (BINV 17)** a For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. > e Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). a ~~a~~ According to WHO, carcinoma of NST encompasses multiple patterns including medullary pattern, cancers with neuroendocrine expression, and other rare patterns. ee Although patients with cancers with 1%–100% ER IHC staining are considered ER-positive and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are more limited data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-low–positive (1%–10%) results. The ER-low–positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus, individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decisionmaking. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). gg Adjuvant Endocrine ± CDK/4/6 Inhibitor Therapy and Principles of Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy (BINV-K). h ~~h~~ Preoperative/Adjuvant Therapy Regimens (BINV-M). > ii The benefit of HER2-targeted therapy is uncertain in HER2-positive breast cancer with pT1a–b, N0 tumors, a population not studied in randomized trials. The decision for use of trastuzumab therapy in this cohort must balance with the known toxicities. jj The absolute benefit of HER2-targeted systemic therapy is negligible in patients with HR-positive cancers and tumor size bordering on T1mic (<1 mm), and endocrine therapy remains a viable option for systemic treatment. kk Consider adjuvant bisphosphonate therapy for risk reduction of distant metastasis for 3–5 years in postmenopausal patients (natural or induced) with high-risk node-negative or node-positive tumors. ll Consider extended adjuvant neratinib following adjuvant trastuzumab-containing therapy for patients with HR-positive, HER2-positive disease with a perceived high risk of recurrence. The benefit or toxicities associated with extended neratinib in patients who have received pertuzumab or ado-trastuzumab emtansine is unknown. mm Updated results from the adjuvant APHINITY trial in HER2-positive early breast cancer, with a median follow-up of 11.3 years, have confirmed the benefit of adding pertuzumab to trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in preventing recurrences. **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** **BINV-5** Version 2.2026, 02/27/26 © 2026 National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ), All rights reserved. **Adjuvant chemotherapy[hh] followed by endocrine therapy ± ovarian suppression/ ablation[gg,kk] (category 1). Consider adjuvant abemaciclib or ribociclib and/or adjuvant olaparib if germline** _**BRCA1/2**_ **PV for eligible patients. See BINV-K 3 of 4 and BINV-M for eligibility criteria.** 

**Adjuvant whole breast RT (BINV-2) or PMRT (BINV-3) as indicated. See BINV-I for sequencing of systemic therapy and RT and - Follow-up (BINV 17)**

k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). a ~~a~~ According to WHO, carcinoma of NST encompasses multiple patterns including medullary pattern, cancers with neuroendocrine expression, and other rare patterns. ee Although patients with cancers with 1%–100% ER IHC staining are considered ER-positive and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are more limited data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-low–positive (1%–10%) results. The ER-low–positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decisionmaking. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). ff Definition of Menopause (BINV-O). 

g ~~g~~ Adjuvant Endocrine ± CDK/4/6 Inhibitor Therapy and Principles of Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy (BINV-K). h ~~h~~ Preoperative/Adjuvant Therapy Regimens (BINV-M). 

- kk  Consider adjuvant bisphosphonate therapy for risk reduction of distant metastasis for 3–5 years in postmenopausal patients (natural or induced) with high-risk node-negative or node-positive tumors. 

nn There are few data regarding the role of gene expression assays in those with ≥4 ipsilateral axillary lymph nodes. Decisions to administer adjuvant chemotherapy for this group should be based on clinical factors. 

qq In premenopausal patients with recurrence score <26, the addition of chemotherapy to endocrine therapy was associated with a lower rate of distant recurrence compared with endocrine monotherapy, but it is unclear if the benefit was due to the ovarian suppression effects promoted by chemotherapy. 

rr Gene Expression Assays for Consideration of Adjuvant Systemic Therapy (BINV-N).

**Consider adjuvant endocrine <1 cm therapy[gg] for risk reduction pT1, pT2, or pT3; and pN0 or pN1mi Consider adjuvant endocrine 1–2.9 cm (≤2 mm axillary therapy[gg] node metastasis) ER-positive • Pure tubular and/or ≥3 cm Adjuvant endocrine therapy[gg,kk] Adjuvant whole • Pure mucinous breast RT (BINV-2) PR-positive, • Pure cribriform or PMRT (BINV-3) HER2-negative[dd] as indicated. pN+ (≥1 ipsilateral Adjuvant endocrine therapy[gg,][kk] See BINV-I for metastases >2 mm) ± adjuvant chemotherapy[a,hh] sequencing of systemic therapy • Adenoid cystic and RT (conventional), and secretory - Follow-up (BINV 17) carcinoma, and ER-negative Limited available data support local therapy other salivary and only with consideration for systemic/targeted carcinomas PR-negative, therapies only in pN+ disease • Rare lowHER2-negative[dd] grade forms of metaplastic carcinoma[bb]** 

a For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). 

bb There are rare subtypes of metaplastic carcinoma (eg, low-grade adenosquamous and low-grade fibromatosis-like carcinoma) that are considered to have a favorable prognosis without adjuvant systemic therapies. 

cc To be associated with favorable prognosis, the favorable histologic type should not be high grade, should be pure (>90% as classified on the surgical excision, not core biopsy alone), and should be HER2 negative. If atypical pathologic or clinical features are present, consider treating as ductal/NST. 

dd Correlation of histology, HR, and HER2 status should always be done with awareness of unusual/discordant or borderline results. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). 

gg Adjuvant Endocrine ± CDK/4/6 Inhibitor Therapy and Principles of Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy (BINV-K). hh Preoperative/Adjuvant Therapy Regimens (BINV-M). 

kk Consider adjuvant bisphosphonate therapy for risk reduction of distant metastasis for 3–5 years in postmenopausal patients (natural or induced) with high-risk node-negative or node-positive tumors. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.**

**ADDITIONAL WORKUP[a]** 

**• Axillary assessment with exam** � **Axillary ultrasound unless prior MRI demonstrated negative axillary c≥T2[tt] or cN+ and M0 nodes or** � **Percutaneous biopsy of suspicious nodes[uu] cT1c, cN0 HER2-positive • Complete blood count (CBC) disease • Comprehensive metabolic panel, including liver function tests or (LFTs) and alkaline phosphatase cT1c, cN0 TNBC Additional tests to consider as clinically indicated (For preoperative • Chest diagnostic CT ± contrast systemic therapy criteria, • Abdomen ± pelvis diagnostic CT with contrast or MRI with contrast see BINV-L 1) • Bone scan or sodium fluoride PET/CT (category 2B) • FDG-PET/CT[vv] • Breast MRI[b] (optional), with special consideration for mammographically occult tumors, if not previously done** 

**For operable breast cancers: see Breast and Axillary Evaluation Prior to Preoperative Systemic - Therapy (BINV 13)** 

**For inoperable breast cancers: see Preoperative Systemic - Therapy (BINV 15)** 

a For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. 

> b Breast MRI may be useful for characterizing axillary and/or internal mammary nodal disease. See Principles of Dedicated Breast MRI Testing (BINV-B) and NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Screening and Diagnosis. 

tt If considering preoperative therapy, consider use of a gene expression assay during workup for pre- and postmenopausal patients with cN0 and postmenopausal patients with cN1, operable ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. (Iwata H, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2019;173:123–133; Ma SJ, et al. Breast Cancer Res Treat 2021;189:737–745; Hanson K, et al. J Clin Oncol 2021;39.15_suppl.591; Beitsch PD, et al. J Clin Oncol 2023;41(16_suppl):Abstract 523). 

uu At the time of biopsy of the most suspicious axillary lymph node(s), a marker should be placed to allow for identification and removal at the time of definitive surgery. vv FDG-PET/CT is most beneficial and accurate for advanced disease (stage III) and invasive ductal (compared to lobular) histology, but may be useful in selected circumstances of earlier stage disease (stage IIA disease: cT1cN1, cT2cN0) such as: equivocal CT+ bone scan results; suspicion of undetected nodal and/or distant disease; and treatment response assessment. An FDG-PET/CT may be utilized as an adjunct to, or in lieu of, initial standard staging and may be performed simultaneously with diagnostic CT. Conversely, a bone scan or sodium fluoride PET/CT may not be needed if an upfront FDG-PET/CT clearly indicates consistent findings on both PET and CT components.

header: **OPERABLE DISEASE: BREAST AND AXILLARY EVALUATION PRIOR TO PREOPERATIVE SYSTEMIC THERAPY**
content:
**Prior to preoperative systemic therapy, perform:** 

- **Core biopsy of breast with placement of imagedetectable clips or marker(s), if not previously performed, should be performed prior to preoperative therapy to demarcate the tumor bed** 

- **Axillary imaging with ultrasound or MRI (if not previously done) and** 

- **Biopsy + marker placement recommended of the most suspicious and/or clinically positive axillary lymph node, if not previously done; only the most suspicious node should be marked and retrieved along with SLNs** 

**Preoperative systemic Surgical Treatment and Adjuvant therapy based on HR and HER2 status (BINV-L 1 Therapy After Preoperative Systemic - and BINV-M) Therapy (BINV 14)**

content:
**Adjuvant systemic therapy (complete planned regimen course if not completed preoperatively) (BINV-16) + whole breast RT[t]** 

**BCS possible** 

- **Any cN0, ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to tumor bed[yy]** 

- **BCS with surgical • cN1 and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed[yy] . Comprehensive RNI** 

- **axillary staging - is not routinely indicated** 

- **(BINV D) ± oncoplastic • cN2–3 and ypN0: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed with comprehensive** 

- **reconstruction[p,ww] RNI including any portion of the axilla at risk** 

- **• Any ypN+: Adjuvant whole breast RT ± boost to the tumor bed[yy] with comprehensive RNI including any portion of the axilla at risk** 

**Adjuvant systemic therapy (complete planned regimen course if not completed preoperatively) (BINV-16) ± PMRT[t ] + RNI (after axilla adequately assessed by SLNB, TAD, Mastectomy and surgical or ALND)[xx] - BCS not possible axillary staging (BINV D) • cT1–T3, cN0, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated ± reconstruction[w] • cT1–T3, cN1, and ypN0: PMRT + RNI is not routinely indicated[zz] • cN2–3 and ypN0; any ypN+; cT4 any N: PMRT + comprehensive RNI with inclusion of any portion of the axilla at risk** 

k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). 

> p Includes techniques such as local tissue rearrangement, local flaps, regional flaps, breast reduction, and mastopexy to allow for greater volumes of resection while optimizing aesthetic outcomes in patients undergoing BCS. 

t Principles of Radiation Therapy (BINV-I). 

> ~~w~~ Principles of Breast Reconstruction Following Surgery (BINV-H). 

w ~~w~~ The accurate assessment of in-breast tumor or regional lymph node response to preoperative systemic therapy is difficult, and should include physical examination and performance of imaging studies (mammogram and/or breast ultrasound and/or breast MRI) that were abnormal at the time of initial tumor staging. Selection of imaging methods prior to surgery should be determined by the multidisciplinary team. MRI is more accurate than mammography for assessing tumor response to preoperative therapy. 

xx Among patients shown to be N+ prior to preoperative systemic therapy, SLNB has a >10% false-negative rate when performed after preoperative systemic therapy, which can be improved by marking the most suspicious biopsied node prior to treatment, and at the time of surgery, using dual tracers, obtaining ≥3 sentinel lymph nodes nodes and removal of the marked node (Targeted Axillary Dissection [TAD]). 

yy Strongly consider RT boost for high-risk features (eg, central/medial tumors, tumors ≥2 cm with ≥1 of the following: grade 3, ER-negative, LVI, high residual cancer in the breast, or young age/longevity). 

zz When considering PMRT + RNI for cN1 that has become ypN0 (after preoperative therapy), based on limited followup duration of NSABP B-51 study (Mamounas EP, et al. N Engl J Med 2025;392:2113-2124) and the inability to demonstrate long-term non-inferiority for this subgroup, a discussion of  potential risks of under-treatment should be weighed against the previously established long-term survival benefits of PMRT+RNI, particularly for patients of younger age/longevity with ER-positive tumors, residual disease in the breast, and/or those with multiple traditional clinical pathologic high-risk features. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.**

- m Considerations for Surgical Axillary Staging (BINV-D). 

- p Includes techniques such as local tissue rearrangement, local flaps, regional flaps, breast reduction, and mastopexy to allow for greater volumes of resection while optimizing aesthetic outcomes in patients undergoing BCS. 

- t Principles of Radiation Therapy (BINV-I). ~~u~~ Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). 

- w Principles of Breast Reconstruction Following Surgery (BINV-H). hh Preoperative/Adjuvant Therapy Regimens (BINV-M). 

- ww  The accurate assessment of in-breast tumor or regional lymph node response to preoperative systemic therapy is difficult, and should include physical examination and performance of imaging studies (mammogram and/or breast ultrasound and/or breast MRI) that were abnormal at the time of initial tumor staging. Selection of imaging methods prior to surgery should be determined by the multidisciplinary team. MRI is more accurate than mammography for assessing tumor response to preoperative therapy. 

- aaa  For patients with skin and/or chest wall involvement (T4 non-inflammatory) prior to preoperative systemic therapy, breast conservation may be performed in carefully selected patients based on a multidisciplinary assessment of local recurrence risk. In addition to standard contraindications to breast conservation (BINV-G), exclusion criteria for breast conservation include: inflammatory (T4d) disease before preoperative systemic therapy and incomplete resolution of skin involvement after preoperative systemic therapy.

The benefit or toxicities associated with extended neratinib in patients who have received Pertuzumab or Ado-trastuzumab emtansine is unknown. mm Updated results from the adjuvant APHINITY trial in HER2-positive early breast cancer, with a median follow-up of 11.3 years, have confirmed the benefit of adding pertuzumab to trastuzumab plus chemotherapy in preventing recurrences. bbb Recommendations do not apply to residual DCIS (ypTis). ccc There are no data on sequencing or combining adjuvant pembrolizumab with capecitabine or olaparib in patients who meet criteria for treatment with one or more of these agents. However, their sequential/combined use may be considered given high risk of recurrence in patients with residual disease. **Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** **BINV-16** Version 2.2026, 02/27/26 © 2026 National Comprehensive Cancer Network[®] (NCCN[®] ), All rights reserved. - **History and physical exam 1–4 times per year as clinically appropriate for 5 years, then annually** 

- **Genetic screening:** 

- **Periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic testing indications and referral to genetic counseling as -** 

- **indicated, see NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate** 

- **Postsurgical management:** 

- **Educate, monitor, and refer for lymphedema management, see NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship: Lymphedema** 

- **Breast imaging:** 

- **Mammography every 12 months, beginning 6 months or more after completion of breast-conservation therapy[ddd]** 

- **• Routine imaging of reconstructed breast is not indicated after mastectomy** 

- **For patients with germline mutations or family history of breast cancer, please refer to NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/ -** 

- **Familial High Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate** 

- **Fertility, birth control, and sexual health (see BINV-C) Screening for metastases: • In the absence of clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of recurrent disease, there is no indication for laboratory or imaging studies for metastases screening.** 

- **Post-treatment monitoring:** 

- **Cardiotoxicity monitoring for patients who received leftsided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy. For anthracycline-induced toxicity, see NCCN Guidelines for** 

- **Survivorship** 

- **Provide guidance on risk of comorbidities Endocrine therapy: • Assess and encourage adherence to adjuvant endocrine therapy**

- **Age-appropriate gynecologic screening** 

- **Routine annual pelvis ultrasound is not recommended** 

- **Patients on an aromatase inhibitor or who experience ovarian failure secondary to treatment should have monitoring of bone health with a bone mineral density (BMD) determination at baseline and periodically thereafter[eee]** 

- **Lifestyle: • Evidence suggests that active lifestyle, healthy diet, limited alcohol intake, and achieving and maintaining an ideal body weight (20–25 body mass index [BMI]) may lead to optimal breast cancer outcomes. See NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Risk Reduction** 

- **Menopausal hormone therapy is relatively contraindicated in survivors of hormonally mediated cancers; should be used with caution in those with increased genetic cancer risk. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship** 

- **Psychosocial support:** 

- **Survivors are at elevated risk for fear of recurrence, distress, anxiety, and depression that may persist for many years after diagnosis. Periodic screening and referral to mental health professionals if needed are recommended. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship** 

- **Communication:** 

- **Coordination of care between the primary care physician (PCP) and specialists is encouraged. Additionally, a personalized survivorship treatment plan including personalized treatment summary of possible long-term toxicity and clear follow-up recommendations is recommended. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship** 

- **Engagement: • Patients frequently require follow-up encouragement in order to improve adherence to ongoing screening and medication adherence. See NCCN Guidelines for Patients** 

**Recurrent Disease** 

**- (BINV 18)** 

- ddd  Studies indicate that annual mammograms are the appropriate frequency for surveillance of patients with breast cancer who have had BCS and RT with no clear advantage to shorter interval imaging. Patients should wait 6 to 12 months after the completion of RT to begin their annual mammogram surveillance. Suspicious findings on physical examination or surveillance imaging should warrant immediate follow-up/workup. 

- eee  The use of estrogen, progesterone, or selective ER modulators to treat osteoporosis or osteopenia in patients with breast cancer is discouraged. The use of a bisphosphonate (oral/IV) or denosumab is acceptable to maintain or to improve BMD and reduce risk of fractures in postmenopausal (natural or induced) patients receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy. Optimal duration of either therapy has not been established. Benefits of duration beyond 3 years or optimal duration beyond 3 years is not known. Factors to consider for duration of anti-osteoporosis therapy include BMD, response to therapy, and risk factors for continued bone loss or fracture. There are case reports of spontaneous fractures after denosumab discontinuation. Patients treated with a bisphosphonate or denosumab should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to the initiation of therapy, and should take supplemental calcium and vitamin D.

**Supportive carejjj,kkk**| - a For tools to aid optimal assessment and comprehensive care of older adults, see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. e Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). - f For risk criteria, see NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate. - h Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A) for the NCCN Distress - Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. - fff For the treatment of brain metastases, see NCCN Guidelines for Central Nervous System Cancers. - g ~~gg~~ False-negative ER and/or PR determinations occur, and there may be discordance between the ER and/or PR determination between the primary and metastatic tumor(s). Therefore, endocrine therapy with its low attendant toxicity may be considered in patients with non-visceral or asymptomatic visceral tumors, especially in patients with clinical characteristics predicting for an HR-positive tumor (eg, long disease-free interval, limited sites of recurrence, indolent disease, older age). - hhh In clinical situations where a biopsy cannot safely be obtained but the clinical evidence is strongly supportive of recurrence, treatment may commence based on the ER/PR/ HER2 status of the primary tumor. Since ER/PR and HER2 status can change with treatment and metastatic progression, it may be appropriate to consider repeat testing on new samples in these scenarios if management will change. iii Tumor tissue or plasma-based circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) assays may be used and each of these have benefits and limitations for diagnosis and disease progression. Tissuebased assays have greater sensitivity for some alterations, but ctDNA may reflect tumor heterogeneity more accurately. If one specimen is negative for actionable biomarkers, testing on the alternative specimen can be considered. jjj See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care and NCCN Guidelines for Supportive Care. kkk Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms. Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term disease-free survival (DFS) for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to “no evidence of disease” (NED). For de novo oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases, and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be considered.

**BINV-27**| |---|---|---| e Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). k Special Considerations for Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth) (BINV-J). ee Although patients with cancers with 1%–100% ER IHC staining are considered ER-positive and eligible for endocrine therapies, there are more limited data on the subgroup of cancers with ER-low–positive (1%–10%) results. The ER-low–positive group is heterogeneous with reported biologic behavior often similar to ER-negative cancers; thus, individualized consideration of risks versus benefits of endocrine therapy and additional adjuvant therapies should be incorporated into decision-making. See Principles of Biomarker Testing (BINV-A). kkk Optimal management of oligometastatic stage IV breast cancers remains uncertain. Routine utilization of locoregional treatment of the primary tumor and ablative treatment for metastatic sites have not been demonstrated to improve survival outcomes in prospective trials, however, may reduce the risk of local progression and palliate symptoms. Compared with recurrent oligometastatic disease, patients with de novo oligometastatic cancer have better outcomes and therapeutic decisions should be individualized and discussed in a multidisciplinary context. A large number of case series document long-term DFS for patients with de novo oligometastatic breast cancer who received multimodality therapy leading to NED. For de novo oligometastatic disease with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED status after receipt of optimal multi-agent systemic regimens, limited sites of metastases, bone-only metastases, and young age/longevity), an aggressive multimodality approach involving locoregional therapy of the primary tumor, ablative treatment to all sites of metastases and "adjuvant-like" systemic therapy may offer improved long-term PFS and may be considered. ppp Denosumab, zoledronic acid, or pamidronate (all with calcium and vitamin D supplementation) should be given (category 1) in addition to systemic therapy or endocrine therapy if bone metastasis is present, expected survival is ≥3 months, and renal function is adequate. Patients should undergo a dental examination with preventive dentistry prior to initiation of this therapy. The optimal schedule for zoledronic acid is every 12 weeks. qqq Baseline assessment of bone density is recommended for patients receiving an aromatase inhibitor who are at risk of osteoporosis (eg, age >65, family history, chronic steroids).

header: NCCN Guidelines Index Table of Contents Discussion
**PRINCIPLES OF SURVIVORSHIP FOR THOSE LIVING WITH ADVANCED OR METASTATIC BREAST CANCER**

content:
- The management unresectable, stage IV (M1) or metastatic disease requires a multifaceted approach, encompassing treatment and a comprehensive survivorship plan. 

- For principles of monitoring metastatic disease, see BINV-R. 

- Encourage patients to promptly report any new or worsening symptoms. A comprehensive evaluation is essential, as these symptoms could indicate disease progression, treatment effects, or other unrelated medical issues. 

- Additional health monitoring, management, and immunizations should be performed as appropriate to the prognosis under the care of a PCP. 

- Patients are encouraged to maintain a therapeutic relationship with a PCP throughout their lifetime.

- Regular follow-up discussions about goals of care and care coordination may be necessary to improve both survival and quality of life. Engaging patients in these discussions, including therapy cessation, ensures that care aligns with their values and preferences. 

- Surveillance and follow-up plans should be individualized for all patients, particularly older patients, considering comorbidities, functional status, and life expectancy. Personalization of care is key to managing the complexities of older adults or disease in those with multiple medical comorbidities. 

- Patients should receive multidisciplinary and interprofessional care that seeks to 

- prevent and/or manage morbidities associated with breast cancer or its treatment. 

- Patients often experience multiple transitions between different care settings and health care providers. Coordination between primary care providers and specialists is essential to ensure continuity of care. 

- Addressing all treatment options at each progression, including the option of supportive care alone or hospice care, is appropriate in patients with stage IV disease. 

- Early involvement of a palliative care team should be considered to provide advance care planning, goals of care discussions, care transitions, psychosocial support, and management of cancer and therapy-related symptoms. 

- Routine screening, evaluation, and assessment for supportive care interventions and referrals are necessary. Some of the symptoms are: 

- �Hormone-related symptoms (NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship) 

- �Cognitive dysfunction (NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship) 

- �Peripheral neuropathy 

- �Cancer-related pain (NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain) 

- �Cancer-related fatigue (NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Related Fatigue) �Cancer-related infections (NCCN Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections) 

- �Chemotherapy-induced anemia, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia (NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors) 

- �Immunotherapy-related toxicities (NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities) 

- �Cardiac monitoring for patients treated with anthracyclines or HER2targeted therapy (NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship) 

- �Nausea/vomiting, anorexia, cachexia, diarrhea, dyspnea, malignant wounds, sleep-wake disturbances (NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis and other NCCN Guidelines for Supportive Care)

- Patients should be encouraged to maintain a physically active lifestyle and a healthy diet whenever possible. See NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship. 

- Consider appropriate cancer and preventive health screenings per patient age and sex assigned-at-birth. These decisions should be individualized according to the patients prognosis. 

- • Screen for anxiety, fear of progression, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) related to cancer and its treatment. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management and NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship. 

- Social determinants of health monitoring and referrals � Provide resources and assistance through financial navigators, social workers, or other specified personnel to address financial concerns, employment issues, and family support. Ensuring access to these services can alleviate significant stress for patients. ◊ See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care: Social Support/Resource Management. 

   - Financial Advocacy Services Guidelines (Association of Cancer Care Centers). 

- Financial Toxicity and Cancer Treatment (National Cancer Institute). 

- �See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management. 

- Facilitate connections with consumer groups, support networks, and organizations that advocate for accessible and equitable care. Engagement with these groups can empower patients and provide additional support. 

- For any patients with sexual health concerns or for fertility and birth control in premenopausal patients, see BINV-C.

- Consistent and clear communication while acknowledging uncertainties of 

- disease trajectory and prognosis is crucial. 

- • Treatment goals, survivorship issues, and the specific roles of each provider should be communicated to the patient.
- See NCCN Resources for Patients and Caregivers and Guidelines for

- Address long-term survivorship issues specific to younger patients, such as . psychosocial development and educational or vocational support. These patients may face unique challenges that require targeted interventions. 

- • Living will/end-of-life planning should be discussed. 

**Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.** 

**BINV-28**

- HER2 testing should be performed on all new primary or newly metastatic breast cancers using methodology outlined in the ASCO/CAP HER2 testing guideline.[a] 

- A re-review of the pathology with consideration for repeat or consultative HER2 testing should be made if a grade 1 (any histologic type), pure mucinous, pure tubular, or pure cribriform carcinoma tests HER2-positive.[a] 

- After a negative HER2 test result on initial biopsy sample, consider retesting on subsequent surgical or other additional sample if the initial sample was suboptimal (eg, minimal invasive cancer was present, cold ischemic time or fixation was suboptimal), testing error is expected, additional samples contain higher grade morphologically distinct cancer from the biopsy, to rule out heterogeneity in a high-grade cancer, or if it will otherwise aid in clinical decisionmaking.[a] 

- HER2 immunohistochemistry (IHC) results with a score of 0 should include the specific staining pattern present (0/absent membrane staining or 0+/with membrane staining) per current CAP Breast Cancer Biomarker Reporting Protocol. 

- Pathologists should be encouraged to examine any IHC 0/no staining carefully for any staining at high power, since the distinction between HER2 ( _ERBB2_ ) IHC 0/absent membrane staining, IHC 0+/with membrane staining (faint, partial membrane staining in ≤10%), IHC 1+, or 2+/ISH negative results (on primary or metastatic samples) is currently clinically relevant since patients with metastatic disease may be eligible for treatment targeting non-amplified levels of HER2 expression. 

- Laboratory must participate in a quality assurance accreditation program for HER2 testing. Otherwise, tissue specimen should be sent to an accredited laboratory for testing. Health care systems and providers must cooperate to ensure the highest quality testing. 

a  NCCN endorses the ASCO/CAP HER2 testing guideline. “Principles of HER2 Testing” modified with permission from Wolff AC, Somerfield MR, Dowsett M, et al. Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 testing in breast cancer. Arch Pathol Lab Med 2023;147:993-1000.
